1: The Seven Woes of ch.23 address leaders of the synagogue system Matthew's community has separated from. The Pharisees' authority is acknowledged (sit in Moses's seat, v.2) while their hypocrisy is indicted — a complex posture that suggests Matthew's community still respected Pharisaic Torah-knowledge while rejecting their leadership as exemplary. The specific practices targeted (phylacteries, tassels, titles of honor like Rabbi, Father, Teacher) are precisely the status-markers of the emerging rabbinic class. The woes function as a prophetic indictment speech, modeled on the OT prophetic woe-oracles (Isa 5:8-23, Amos 5-6).
2: "The scribes and the Pharisees sit on Moses' seat" (epi tēs Mōuseōs kathedras ekathisan) — the cathedra (chair of authority) was a literal object in synagogues: a stone seat near the Torah ark from which authoritative interpretation was delivered. Sitting to teach was the formal posture of rabbinic authority (cf. Luke 4:20; John 8:2). The phrase acknowledges that the Pharisees occupy a legitimate institutional position within the Torah-interpretation tradition that descends from Moses.
3: "Do what they say but not what they do" — a startling acknowledgment of institutional authority decoupled from personal exemplification. Jesus does not tell his disciples to ignore Pharisaic teaching in principle but to distinguish between authoritative transmission of Torah content and the lived hypocrisy that undermines it. This verse resists any simple reading of the Woes as anti-Pharisaic wholesale: the issue is practice, not office.
4: "They tie up heavy burdens (phortia barea) and lay them on people's shoulders, but they themselves are not willing to move them with their finger" — the "heavy burdens" refer to the halakhic expansions of the oral tradition, the "fence around the Torah" (m. Avot 1:1 commands: "Make a fence around the Torah"). The Mishnah and Talmud later codified hundreds of specific applications: 39 categories of Sabbath-prohibited work, elaborate purity rules, tithing regulations. The critique is not that rules exist but that the teachers impose without enabling — they do not themselves bear the weight they place on others.
5: Phylacteries (phylaktēria) are the Greek name for the Jewish tefillin — small black leather boxes containing four Torah passages (Exod 13:1-10; 13:11-16; Deut 6:4-9; 11:13-21) bound on the left arm and forehead during morning weekday prayer, fulfilling Deut 6:8 literally. "Broad phylacteries" (platynousin) means making the boxes larger as a visible display of piety. The fringes (kraspeda) are the tzitzit — tassels required by Num 15:38-40 and Deut 22:12 on the four corners of a garment, intended to be visible reminders of the commandments. Lengthening them was another form of public piety display.
6: The place of honor at banquets (prōtoklisian) is the reclining couch at the top of the symposium arrangement, nearest the host. In the Greek and Roman banquet tradition this position was the highest honor, its occupant identifiable to all present. The front seat in synagogues (prōtokathedral) was the bench facing the congregation (and facing toward Jerusalem), reserved for senior members and teachers. Both positions made social rank visible and permanent throughout the gathering.
7: Greetings in the marketplaces and the title "Rabbi" (hrabbi) — the honorific title "Rabbi" (literally "my great one") was in process of formalization during the first century; it became the standard designation for ordained teachers after 70 CE. Public greeting in the agora was a performance of social rank: the higher-status person acknowledged by greetings and titles confirmed community recognition of their position. The desire for such greetings is a desire for public status-validation.
8: "But you are not to be called rabbi, for you have one teacher, and you are all brothers" — the horizontal community structure Jesus commands rejects the vertical title-hierarchy of rabbinic Judaism. "One teacher" (heis estin hymon ho didaskalos) — Jesus himself occupies the teaching role that the rabbinic chain of transmission claimed to occupy. "All brothers" (pantes de hymeis adelphoi este) collapses the hierarchical teacher-student distinction into a sibling relationship under one Father.
9: "And call no one your father on earth, for you have one Father, who is in heaven" — the prohibition on "Father" as a religious title prevents the kind of filial piety relationship that created binding dependence on human teachers. In the rabbinic tradition, one's teacher was spoken of in terms that paralleled one's father — transmission of Torah created a quasi-paternal relationship. Jesus reserves this relationship to God alone, preventing any human teacher from claiming the covenant-father role.
10: "Neither be called instructors (kathēgētai), for you have one instructor, the Christ" — the Greek kathēgētēs designates a guide or leader as well as a teacher. All three prohibited titles (rabbi, father, instructor) are titles that claim interpretive authority over others. Jesus's prohibition restructures the community around his own authority rather than establishing a new human hierarchy to replace the Pharisaic one.
11: "The greatest among you shall be your servant" — the honor-reversal principle of 20:26-27 is restated here in the context of the leadership critique. The greatest (in terms of kingdom status) is determined by service rendered, not titles held. The Pharisees accumulate titles; the kingdom community accumulates service. The two economies of status are structurally incompatible.
12: "Whoever exalts himself will be humbled, and whoever humbles himself will be exalted" — an eschatological inversion principle that appears as a general law in both OT and Jewish wisdom tradition (Prov 29:23; Ezek 21:26; Job 22:29; 1QH 15:15). Jesus frames it as the operating logic of the kingdom: the divine response to human self-promotion is diminishment; to self-lowering, elevation. The Woes that follow illustrate the consequences of the scribes and Pharisees' self-exaltation.
13: The first Woe: "You shut the kingdom of heaven in people's faces" — the scribes and Pharisees as gatekeepers who deny entry through their own unbelief and block others from entering. The word kleiete (you lock, you shut) is the same root used in Matt 16:19 where Peter receives the keys of the kingdom. Those who should be door-openers have become door-closers. The woe (ouai) is the prophetic lament-curse: a declaration of grief and judgment simultaneously.
14: This verse ("you devour widows' houses and for a pretense make long prayers") is absent from the earliest manuscripts (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) and is generally considered a scribal insertion from Mark 12:40 and Luke 20:47. It does not appear in the best manuscript tradition of Matthew and is omitted from modern critical editions. The verse-numbering gap in modern translations reflects its absence.
15: The second Woe: "You travel across sea and land to make a single proselyte, and when he becomes a proselyte, you make him twice as much a child of hell as yourselves" — the Pharisaic proselytizing mission is attested in Roman sources (Horace, Sat. 1.4.143; Roman expulsion of Jews 139 BCE partly for proselytizing). The charge is not that making converts is wrong but that the proselyte is formed in the teachers' defects, inheriting their distorted piety and becoming an intensified version of it.
16: The third Woe (vv. 16-22) targets oath-taking distinctions in halakhic debate. "Blind guides (hodēgoi typhloi) who say, 'If anyone swears by the temple, it is nothing, but if anyone swears by the gold of the temple, he is bound by his oath'" — the Mishnah tractate Nedarim and Shevuot contain extensive discussions about which oaths are binding and which can be released. Swearing by an object that can be given to the temple vs. by the temple itself created a legal distinction between binding and non-binding vows.
17: "You blind fools! For which is greater, the gold or the temple that has made the gold sacred?" — the logical critique of halakhic oath distinctions is that they reverse the order of sanctity. The gold derives its sacred status from the temple (the context of consecration), not the other way around. To treat the consecrating object (gold) as more binding than the consecrating context (temple) is to confuse derivative and primary sanctity.
18: "And you say, 'If anyone swears by the altar, it is nothing, but if anyone swears by the gift that is on the altar, he is bound by his oath'" — a parallel oath distinction: the altar vs. the sacrifice on it. The same logical problem arises: the sacrifice derives its sacred status from the altar of consecration. The halakhic ruling has confused what makes something holy with the holy thing itself.
19: "You blind men! For which is greater, the gift or the altar that makes the gift sacred?" — the repetition of the blind-fools charge with the same logical structure as v.17 reinforces the critique: this is not an isolated error but a pattern of reasoning that consistently gets the hierarchy of sanctity backwards. The "blind" epithet recurs throughout the Woes (v.16, 17, 19, 24, 26) as the defining diagnosis.
20: "Therefore, whoever swears by the altar swears by it and by everything on it" — Jesus collapses the halakhic distinction by showing that swearing by the altar necessarily includes the gift on it (since the altar's purpose is the sacrifice) and vice versa. Any meaningful oath by the altar implicates the full sacrificial complex. The attempt to use definitional distinctions to escape the weight of oaths is exposed as illogical.
21: "And whoever swears by the temple swears by it and by him who dwells in it" — the temple is YHWH's dwelling place (1 Kgs 8:10-13; Ezek 43:4-7); any oath by the temple necessarily invokes the one who inhabits it. The attempt to swear by the building while avoiding the divine presence it houses is incoherent: the building's entire significance derives from the divine indwelling.
22: "And whoever swears by heaven swears by the throne of God and by him who sits upon it" — the escalation to heaven makes the point absolutely: all oaths ultimately invoke God, regardless of what object is nominally named. No human-devised distinction can create an oath that does not implicate divine witness. The elaborate halakhic distinctions about oath-bindingness are shown to be not just logically confused but theologically evasive.
23: The fourth Woe: "You tithe mint and dill and cummin, and have neglected the weightier matters of the law: justice and mercy and faithfulness" — the Mishnah tractate Maasrot (4:5) explicitly lists herbs including mint (mentha), dill (anethon), and cummin (kuminon) as subject to tithe. Tithing garden herbs to the last leaf was technically correct Torah-observance (Lev 27:30 requires tithing "everything from the land"). The critique is not that this is wrong but that "the weightier matters of the law" — the prophetic priorities of justice (mishpat), mercy (chesed), and faithfulness (emunah) — have been squeezed out by the focus on small-scale technical compliance.
24: "You blind guides, straining out a gnat and swallowing a camel!" — gnats (kōnōpes) were Levitically impure creatures (Lev 11:41-42) that could contaminate wine if consumed. Straining wine through a cloth to remove gnats was a common purity practice. The camel (kamēlos) is the largest Levitically impure animal (Lev 11:4). The hyperbole is deliberate and comic: the contrast between the smallest and largest impure creature perfectly captures the disproportionality of Pharisaic purity-focus — meticulous about tiny impurities while consuming massive ones.
25: The fifth Woe: "You clean the outside of the cup and the plate, but inside they are full of greed and self-indulgence" — the purity of vessels was an elaborate concern in rabbinic law: the Mishnah tractate Kelim (Vessels) contains thirty chapters addressing the impurity-susceptibility of various containers based on material, shape, and use. The cup that is ritually pure on the outside while full of extortion is a precise image of formal religious compliance masking moral corruption.
26: "First clean the inside of the cup and the plate, that the outside also may be clean" — the metaphor addresses priorities in the order of moral transformation: the source of action (the interior character) must be addressed before external behavior can be genuinely changed. External purity practice derived from internal greed produces only a surface result. The reversal of the typical ritual focus (outside → inside) mirrors the reversal of the typical honor economy (visible → hidden).
27: The sixth Woe: "You are like whitewashed tombs, which outwardly appear beautiful, but within are full of dead people's bones and all uncleanness" — tombs were whitewashed with lime before Passover (m. Shekalim 1:1; m. Maasrot Sheni 5:1; b. Moed Katan 5a) specifically to warn pilgrims traveling to Jerusalem: contact with a grave or its immediate area caused seven days of corpse-impurity (Num 19:16), which would prevent participation in the Passover. The whitewash made graves visible and beautiful — a public safety marker that also beautified dangerous defilement. The image is precise: the Pharisees' public appearance is both beautiful and specifically designed to warn others away from the defilement within.
28: "Outwardly you appear righteous to others, but within you are full of hypocrisy and lawlessness" (anomias) — the diagnosis is not that the Pharisees are outwardly wicked but that their outward righteousness is a performance divorced from interior reality. Hypocrisis in Greek originally meant stage-acting (playing a role). The actors wear the mask of righteousness; inside is the opposite. The antithesis between external appearance and internal reality is the structural center of the Woes.
29: The seventh Woe: "You build the tombs of the prophets and decorate the monuments of the righteous" — the veneration of prophets' tombs was widespread in Second Temple Judaism. Josephus refers to commemorative structures for the Maccabean family (Ant. 13.211); the Tomb of Zechariah in the Kidron Valley (traditionally dated to the Second Temple period) and similar monumental tomb-structures were religious sites. The building and decorating of such tombs was an act of piety toward those who died for faithfulness to God — or so it appeared.
30: "And you say, 'If we had lived in the days of our fathers, we would not have taken part with them in shedding the blood of the prophets'" — the self-distancing from the fathers who killed the prophets is both sincere (they genuinely believe they would have acted differently) and ironic (they are in the act of plotting to kill Jesus). The historical distance that allows comfortable condemnation of the fathers does not translate into behavioral change when the living prophetic word confronts them directly.
31: "Thus you witness against yourselves that you are sons of those who murdered the prophets" — the testimony is their own statement: by identifying themselves as the children of the prophet-killers, they claim the lineage. In the deuteronomistic tradition, Israel's characteristic sin is the rejection and killing of prophets (2 Chr 36:15-16; Neh 9:26; Jer 7:25-26). To claim descent from that tradition is to claim participation in it.
32: "Fill up, then, the measure of your fathers" (plērōsate to metron tōn paterōn hymōn) — this ironic command anticipates the completion of the pattern: Jesus' death will fill the measure of prophet-rejection that began with Abel. The "measure" (metron) is a capacity that has been building through generations and will be filled by the act they are about to commit.
33: "You serpents, you brood of vipers, how are you to escape being sentenced to hell?" — the "brood of vipers" epithet connects to John the Baptist's identical charge (3:7), placing the Pharisees in a continuous line of covenant resistance that the Baptist had already identified. The rhetorical question — "how are you to escape?" — is unanswerable given the preceding analysis: they are repeating the pattern of their fathers with full self-awareness.
34: "Therefore I send you prophets and wise men and scribes, some of whom you will kill and crucify, and some you will flog in your synagogues and persecute from town to town" — Jesus identifies himself as the sender of God's messengers, claiming the divine role in the prophetic-martyrdom tradition. The list of measures (killing, crucifying, flogging, persecuting) describes the treatment of the early Christian community documented in Acts and Paul's letters, making this a prospective judgment-oracle on the community's response to the apostolic mission.
35: "So that on you may come all the righteous blood shed on earth, from the blood of righteous Abel to the blood of Zechariah the son of Berechiah, whom you murdered between the sanctuary and the altar" — the span from Abel (Gen 4:8, the first murder in Scripture) to Zechariah covers the full Hebrew canon: in the Hebrew Bible's order, Genesis is the first book and Chronicles the last; Zechariah son of Jehoiada was killed "in the court of the house of the LORD" (2 Chr 24:21), making him the last martyr recorded in the canon. This is a merism — all prophetic blood from first to last in the biblical narrative.
36: "Truly, I say to you, all these things will come upon this generation" — the judgment announced is not deferred to the eschaton but specified as falling on "this generation" (tautēn tēn genean). This language, used consistently in Matthew (12:39, 41-42; 17:17; 23:36; 24:34), refers to the contemporaries of Jesus who are rejecting his ministry. The Woes culminate in a judgment-oracle whose realization is imminent.
37: "O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets and stones those who are sent to it! How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing!" — the "gathering under wings" image for divine protection occurs throughout the Psalms (17:8; 36:7; 57:1; 61:4; 91:4) and Ruth 2:12 ("under whose wings you have come to take refuge"). The image of YHWH as a bird gathering Israel is maternal and tender; its frustration — "you were not willing" (ouk ēthelēsate) — is the climactic statement of Israel's covenant resistance.
38: "See, your house is left to you desolate" (aphietai hymin ho oikos hymōn erēmos) — the "house" is the temple. The pronouncement echoes Jeremiah's Temple Sermon (Jer 7:1-15), where YHWH threatens to abandon the temple as he abandoned Shiloh, and Ezekiel's vision of the divine glory departing from the temple (Ezek 8-11). "Your house" rather than "my house" signals the withdrawal of divine ownership: the temple has reverted to being merely Israel's building, no longer YHWH's dwelling.
39: "For I tell you, you will not see me again, until you say, 'Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord'" — the citation of Ps 118:26 (the messianic acclamation of the triumphal entry, 21:9) frames the departure and future return as a bracket around a period of absence. Jesus' departure from the temple precincts is presented as a hiatus, not a final end; the messianic acclamation will be heard again at the parousia. The lament closes with a conditional future, not despair.
